---
title: "MCU knockout: mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake"
subtitle: "Platelet whole-cell model — demo experiment 3 of 4"
author: "Steve Haigh"
date: 2026-06-23
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    geometry: "margin=2.5cm"
    fontsize: 11pt
    mainfont: "STIX Two Text"
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: blue
    urlcolor: blue
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    include-in-header: ../pandoc-header.tex
  html:
    toc: true
    embed-resources: true
---

## Purpose

Knock out the **mitochondrial calcium uniporter (MCU)** on the
[baseline experiment](1-baseline-resting-agonist.qmd) and observe its effect on
the cytosolic Ca²⁺ transient. This is the experiment where the model makes a
**counterintuitive, emergent, literature-matched** prediction.

## What is changed from the baseline

Exactly **one** knob relative to experiment 1:

| | Baseline | MCU knockout |
|---|---|---|
| `mcu_vmax_scale` | 1.0 | **0.0** |

Everything else is identical (Dolan agonist at 60 s, +Ca²⁺, 300 s). In the TUI
this is the **MCU V_max → KO** checkbox. `mcu_vmax_scale` scales the maximal MCU
flux, so `0.0` removes mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake entirely.

## Biological reasoning

The MCU is the main channel that lets cytosolic Ca²⁺ enter the mitochondrial
matrix. During platelet activation, mitochondria **take up** a share of the
cytosolic Ca²⁺ that floods in from the store — they act as a transient
**buffer/sink** that shapes the cytosolic signal (and, at high load, drive the
procoagulant response). Naively, one might expect that removing a Ca²⁺-*uptake*
pathway would have little effect on, or even lower, cytosolic Ca²⁺.

## What happens in the model

The opposite happens: knocking out MCU **raises** the cytosolic Ca²⁺ peak from
**429 nM to 602 nM (+40 %)** and the sustained plateau likewise (423 → 600 nM)
(@fig-mcu). Crucially, the DTS store still empties to ~1 µM in **both** cases —
mitochondria do not refill the store; they merely siphon Ca²⁺ out of the
cytosol. Remove that siphon and more Ca²⁺ remains free in the cytosol. The
slightly higher integrin activation (PAC-1⁺ 0.796 → 0.842) and thromboxane that
follow are simply downstream of the larger Ca²⁺ signal.

![**Cytosolic Ca²⁺ with MCU knocked out (current) vs the baseline (grey).** Left: removing mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake raises the cytosolic peak/plateau by ~40 % (429 → 602 nM) — the curve sits *above* the grey wild-type baseline. Centre: the DTS store empties to ~1 µM in both runs (grey and current overlap at the bottom), confirming mitochondria redistribute Ca²⁺ away from the cytosol rather than returning it to the store; store-operated entry (right axis) is similar. Right: IP₃ is essentially unchanged (the perturbation is downstream of IP₃). The baseline's duration is shown in its legend label.](../figures/demos/mcu_calcium.png){#fig-mcu width=100%}

This result is **emergent**: the model was never tuned to produce it. It falls
out of having mitochondria, the store and the pumps all present and
mass-balanced — the payoff of a whole-cell rather than single-pathway model. It
is also the *one* perturbation in this set that visibly moves the otherwise
store-limited cytosolic Ca²⁺ peak, because mitochondrial uptake competes for
cytosolic Ca²⁺ directly during the transient.

## What happens in real platelets

MCU-deficient (MCU⁻/⁻) platelets show **elevated** agonist-evoked cytosolic
Ca²⁺ — exactly because the mitochondrial buffer is gone — together with reduced
mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload and a blunted procoagulant (phosphatidylserine-
exposure) response (Ghatge et al. 2026, and related work). The *direction* of the
cytosolic-Ca²⁺ change is the headline phenotype, and it is the one the model
reproduces.

## Honest comparison

- **What the model gets right.** The counterintuitive *rise* in cytosolic Ca²⁺
  on MCU loss, and the mechanism (mitochondria buffer/redistribute cytosolic
  Ca²⁺ rather than rescue the store) — emergent and matching the MCU⁻/⁻ phenotype
  the model was not fitted to. This is the strongest "the model predicted
  something" result in the demo set.
- **Magnitude.** The +40 % is a model number. There is no clean wet-lab figure
  for the fold-change to fit against, so only the direction should be claimed
  quantitatively.
- **What the model cannot show.** MCU's *other* major role — driving the
  mitochondrial-Ca²⁺ overload that triggers the procoagulant/PS-exposure
  response — is absent: the model has a mitochondrial Ca²⁺ pool (MCU/NCLX) but no
  bioenergetics and no procoagulant pathway. So it captures the Ca²⁺-buffering
  role of MCU, not its procoagulant role.
- **Single-cell.** As always, aggregation and any thrombus-level consequence are
  out of reach.

## Take-away

MCU knockout is the demo that best showcases the model's value: a perturbation
whose result (cytosolic Ca²⁺ goes *up*) is not obvious from the knob turned, is
emergent from the integrated Ca²⁺ machinery, and matches a named knockout
phenotype the model was never tuned to.
